observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the CAD to prepare and maintain the SLCs for each type of inventory items pursuant to Section 120, Chapter 7 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: In maintaining of the SLC, the CAD currently records each inventory transaction manually whenever there is a movement. However, this manual process is time-consuming and resource intensive. To address these challenges, the CAD, in coordination with the CGSD and with the assistance of SPIDC, plans to implement the General Service Management System (GSMS). This automated system aims to improve efficiency, reduce human error, provide real-time inventory data, and minimize the tedious tasks associated with manual tracking.
observations.reason:
Due to technical readiness and infrastructure limitations cause the challenges, particularly in ensuring compatibility with existing systems and ensuring data security. 

Furthermore, the need for staff training and change management to transition from a manual to an automated system requires careful scheduling and resource allocation, further contributing to the delay.

observations.recommendation:

GSD to conduct physical count of all PPE, including land, land improvements, infrastructure assets, buildings, and other structures, the results of which shall be reflected in the Reports on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and reconciled with the accounting records; and

CAD to maintain an updated property ledger cards for each PPE item, and the CGSD to maintain the equivalent property cards to properly account for the receipt and disposition of each PPE item.

We also recommended that Management adopt and fully implement the “One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances” as authorized under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to address the unreconciled discrepancies between the accounting and property records, subject to full compliance with the general and procedural guidelines provided therein.

We recommended that Management direct the: 

GSD to: 

Prepare the corresponding Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) for the reported unserviceable properties, after which, proceed with the disposal procedures in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992; 

After the disposal, completely provide the necessary details on the Inspection and Disposal portion of the IIRUP, duly certified by the inspection officer and the witness, and submit the original copy of the report to the CAD for the preparation of the corresponding JEV for the derecognition of the book value of the properties from the books of accounts; and

Design an efficient and effective disposal plan/schedule to cover all unserviceable properties and prevent them from remaining undisposed by year-end.

CAD to draw the corresponding JEV for the derecognition of the properties disposed of based on the accomplished IIRUP submitted by the CGSD.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Intensify efforts in retrieving relevant documents from the turned-over files of the former City Treasurer/s and City Accountant/s relating to the unrecognized/unrecorded bank debit entries;
If warranted, issue a demand letter to the former City Treasurer/s and City Accountant/s to explain the cause/s of the unrecognized/unrecorded bank debit entries;
Issue a follow-up letter to the banks concerned for information/documents on the details of these bank debits; and
Effect the necessary adjustment/s on the books of accounts, upon availability or receipt of the relevant documents from the banks concerned.

b) CIB-LCCA and LCSA totaling P56,651,041.78 remain dormant for more than eight years, contrary to DOF-DBM-COA JC No. 4-2012 dated September 11, 2012, thus, the reliability of the CIB account balance to the extent of this amount cannot be ascertained.
We recommended that Management require the CAD and CTD to:
Ensure that the City's cash accounts are properly supported with confirmed bank balances and relevant documentation;
Reconcile the City's records with the records of the source agencies (SAs) about the dormant trust fund accounts to facilitate the return or disposition of the balances to the SAs concerned;
Effect the necessary adjustment/s in the books of accounts, as may be warranted, upon receipt of the relevant documents from the banks concerned and/or as indicated in the supporting transaction documents; and
Review all existing bank accounts and monitor on a regular basis their status to ensure that they remain active and relevant to the present programs and projects of the City, otherwise, cause the closure, transfer or other appropriate action.

13 bank accounts with a total amount of P76,927,901.34 were not backed up with bank confirmations, contrary to Section 13, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Section 111 of PD No. 1445, and without supporting documents, hence, affecting the reliability of the reported balances of the CIB accounts in the financial statements.
We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Intensify efforts to retrieve any relevant documents from the turned-over files of the former City Treasurer/s and City Accountant/s as basis of further action;
Issue a demand letter to the identified signatories requesting information/explanation on the relevant supporting documents of the non-existing bank accounts; and
Issue a follow-up request, indicating the names of the signatories, with the banks concerned for documents or information, copy furnished the head offices, for the possibility of securing related archived documents/information.

17 out of the 37 cash accounts with a total balance of P97,707,292.13 had no BRS prepared as of December 31, 2024, contrary to Sections 3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 12, 1996, due to absence of Bank Statements from the banks concerned, hence, affected the accuracy and verifiability of the Cash balances.
We recommended that Management require the CAD to prepare the BRS for all bank accounts and coordinate with the banks concerned, the accounts that are dormant or for which statements have not been provided, in compliance with Sections 3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011.

Stale Outstanding Checks in the total amount of P1,392,112.38 from CYs 2023 and 2024 remain part of the bank reconciling items in the BRS for DBP for not complying with the requirements of Section 59, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, thus understating the Cash balance at year-end.
We recommended that Management require the CTD to:
Immediately send another set of written notice/s to the payee/s of the existence of the stale check/s; and
Henceforth, strictly monitor all outstanding checks to ensure compliance with Section 59, Chapter 3, of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Strictly enforce the liquidation of CAs granted to AOs within the reglementary period in compliance with Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
Impose the sanctions provided in Section 5.9 of the same COA Circular, as warranted by the circumstances.

We recommended that Management require the:
CAD to substantiate the request for write-off in the amount of P883,053.05, strictly complying the requirements of COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and
The City Administrator, to constantly remind officials and employees concerned of the pertinent laws, rules, and regulations governing the handling of CAs every time they were granted with CA.

We recommended that Management require the CGSD, CHD, CCMC, and CCNMC to:
Conduct periodic physical counts of inventory on hand and reconciliation of inventory records with actual inventory in order to identify and correct any discrepancies;
Comply with the requirement to notify the OCA on the conduct of physical count pursuant to Section 157 of COA Circular No. 92-386; and
Require all supply and property officers/custodians to prepare the SSMI in the prescribed format for submission to CAD.

We recommended that Management require the CAD and the CGSD to reconcile their records and adjust accordingly, and to conduct regular reconciliations to prevent further accumulation of variances.

We recommended that Management require the CGSD to check their records and reclassify the identified PPEs to the correct account.

We recommended that Management require the Caloocan City Medical Center (CCMC) and Caloocan City North Medical Center (CCNMC) to provide copy of the necessary donation documents to the CAD and CGSD for proper recording and updating of property records.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to record all donated PPE identified during the one-time cleansing activities and ensure that complete supporting documents are provided for any future PPE donations to any office/department of the City.

We recommended that Management require the CAD and the CGSD to ensure a thorough reconciliation process and prioritize the completion of the reconciliation as part of the ongoing one-time cleansing.
We further recommended that Management instruct the CGSD to expedite the issuance of demand letters against personnel responsible for missing PPE items.

We recommended that Management require the HRMDO to:
Streamline the existing process by eliminating redundancies and introduce a centralized system, in coordination with the originating office, Budget Office, and other offices involved, to enhance efficient service delivery; and
Review the current capability of the HRMIS to possibly include additional features thereby maximizing its use.

We commended Management and the responsible officials for the prompt and high utilization of the SPISC funds, in pursuance of the objectives of RA No. 9994, as amended by RA No. 11916, and DSWD MC No. 06, s. 2024.

We further recommended that Management require the OSCA to maintain these good practices and continuously seek suitable solutions to completely distribute the SPISC payouts to all eligible beneficiaries in full attainment of the objectives of the program.

We commended Management for faithfully adhering to the provisions of Section 287 of RA No. 7160 and the related DILG-DBM joint issuances in implementing 86 out of 90 planned socio-economic and environmental development projects.

As to the four (4) unimplemented projects, we recommended that Management require the City Planning and Development Office, in coordination with the CED, to continue to monitor the status and the eventual implementation of the targeted development projects funded out of the 20% DF.

We commended the City for continuously complying with the provisions of Section 1, Rule 18 of the IRR of RA No. 10121 in setting aside five percent of the budget intended for programs, projects and activities supporting disaster risk management activities; and
We recommended the sustained commitment by the City in complying with the five percent requirement.

We commended the Management for being a vibrant lead for the timely and full implementation of its LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the purpose of the fund and to carry out its vision for an effective and efficient mechanism for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response rehabilitation and recovery, aligned with the dynamics of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 in pursuit of the objectives of Section 21 of RA No. 10121; and
We recommended that the City intensify its efforts as a vibrant and proactive lead in the identification and implementation of DRRM-related PPAs capturing the four DRRM thematic areas.

We commended the Management in maintaining a duly constituted and functioning GFPS by espousing the continues conduct of capacity development programs aimed at providing updates and enhancing the GFPS skills and capabilities, and periodic monitoring and evaluation of GFPS accomplishments to address possible and identified issues that may hinder the effective performance of their functions in accordance with the MCW and other GAD-related laws, policies and plans; and
We recommended that Management unceasingly support its GFPS in the exercise of its role and in enhancing its potentials to foster the goal of RA No. 9710.

We recommended that Management require the City’s GFPS to:
Prioritize the preparation of the draft GAD Agenda; and
Strictly comply with the preparation of GAD Agenda covering a three-year period pursuant to Item C.2, Paragraph 1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We commended the Management for allocating more than five percent of its annual budget for GAD PPAs, ensuring the availability of enough budget to support the implementation of the City’s GAD PPAs; and
We recommended that Management intensify its campaign for gender mainstreaming in regular PPAs to gain increased GAD budget allocation as envisioned under RA No. 9710.

We recognized the efforts of Management and its responsible officials for the high percentage of implementation of GAD PPAs utilizing 97.02 percent totaling P641,162,562.14 of the allocated GAD funds. Nevertheless, we recommended that the City GAD Focal Point closely coordinate with the implementing offices to consider in their succeeding proposals the challenges that affected their implementation in the past year to be able to fully implement programmed GAD PPAs based on the DILG-endorsed GAD Plan and Budget, moving forward.
We recommended that Management establish their own responsibility accounting by creating or assigning RCs and RC Codes in compliance with COA Circular No. 2021-008 dated September 06, 2021.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Resource and Coordinating Office to observe timely submission of the DILG-endorsed GPB and revised GPB, as well as the GAD AR to the Office of the City Auditor within the prescribed period to facilitate evaluation and validation of the implementation of GAD PPAs and the utilization of GAD funds.

We recommended that the Management require the CEMD with the assistance of all Punong Barangays to:
Provide properly labeled waste bins available in the streets, public areas, and conspicuous areas within the City;
Strictly ensure that wastes are only placed on the designated collection points during the collection schedule; and
Heighten strict enforcement of City Ordinance No. 0407, s. 2006, and intensify proactive strategies and efforts in the implementation of the SWM programs of the City in accordance with Section 21 of RA No. 9003.

We acknowledged the efforts of Management and its responsible officials in assisting the establishment of MRF/MRS in every barangay within the City. Nevertheless, we recommended that Management, through the CEMD, to:
Strictly monitor the operations of the MRF/MRS, and regularly visit these facilities, to ensure that the same are being used as intended; and
Implement the prescribed procedures and guidelines provided in RA No. 9003 for the establishment of MRFs in barangays and cluster of barangays that have not yet established them.

We recommended that the Management direct the CSWD to:
Strictly monitor the implementation of PPAs in accordance with the timeline set to ensure the timely delivery of the services to the intended beneficiaries and compliance with the requirements for the procurement;
Establish base-criteria in allocating the funds only to those PPAs for strengthening of the LCPC which are included in the enumeration pursuant to RA No. 9344 and DILG MC No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012; and
Perform meticulous planning and implementation of all identified and budgeted CCCPC PPAs to ensure that all aspects of child protection, including prevention, intervention, and support services, are thoroughly addressed, leaving no gaps in safeguarding children's rights and well-being, in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9344.

We commended the Management for submitting the soft copies of their CY 2024 AIPs with CCET and QAR to the LGU CCET Help Desk showing their commitment in maintaining transparency and increased effectiveness of climate change expenditures tagging and tracking of the City; and
We recommended continues compliance of the City in the preparation and submission of the QAR with the AIP pursuant to JMC No. 2015-01.

Management comments/actions:
Management in its Letter dated April 14, 2025, acknowledged the observation and emphasized the corrective actions and institutional mechanisms that are currently being implemented, viz:
Ensured that the regular annual capacity-building activities are conducted by the CPDD for concerned personnel involved in CCET, to strengthen internal competencies in using the Climate Change Typology and alignment with current standards;
Has adopted the guidance for the CY 2026 AIP preparation cycle based on the recent technical training facilitated by the CCC on April 2 to 3, 2025. During the training, LGUs were advised to reduce the number of tagged PPAs and focus exclusively on activities with direct and measurable climate relevance; and
Has initiated an internal review system to further evaluate the technical consistency of tagged entries before submission.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City of Caloocan Climate Change Action Plan (CCCCAP) Core Team oversee the process of tagging climate-responsive PPAs in the LGU Plans and Investment Programs to ensure their proper identification and prioritization with the help of an expert/specialist on climate change from its members, or hired externally, as necessary, and henceforth comply with the DBM-CCC-DILG MC No. 2015-01, dated July 23, 2015, in crafting the AIP using the CCET as guide.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Remit in full the amount of taxes withheld within the timeframe to avoid the incurrence of penalties that may be imposed by the BIR; and
Review and improve existing procedures to forestall the occurrence of delays on remittances.

We appreciated Management’s efforts and encourage sustained compliance with the provisions of Section 6 (b) of RA No. 8291, Section 3 (a) of the IRR of RA No. 9679 and the updated PhilHealth Payment Schedule per Advisory No. 04-04-2014, and NHIA Circular No. 0001, s. 2014.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Intensify efforts to reconcile the records to establish the correct amount for remittance to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth;
Continue to communicate with PhilHealth and explore ways to resolve the issue on the December 1999 contribution reported in the amount of P223,575.00; and
Evaluate the advice of the CLD and take necessary action without delay.

We recommended that Management require the CGSD, CED and Procurement Office to:
Submit the copies of all contracts, POs and NODs with the supporting documents within the prescribed period to facilitate the review and evaluation of the contracts and POs and to conduct inspection of delivered items pursuant to existing regulations; and
Re-examine the existing process and develop effective strategies to address the causes of delay in the submission of contracts, POs and NODs.

observations.managementAction:
Strict monitoring of updating membership record of employees with the GSIS through monthly coordination with the HRMDO for the listing of clarificatory items. 

In order to maintain a reconciled record with the GSIS, we have initiated a request from the HRMDO to furnish our office a copy of notices such as Notice of Salary Increment/ Adjustment, notice of separation, newly hired and appointed employees, which are relevant to verify employees’ record with the GSIS before remittances of compulsory premiums. Also, the Remittance Division of our office decided to perform a pre-audit of payrolls with regard to checking of GSIS withholdings since February 2024 as of this time. This procedure was included to check, verify and reconcile employees’ GSIS record with the amount of contributions deducted in the payrolls and to ensure that the remitted amount is the reconciled with the GSIS monthly billing statement.

For PhilHealth – the CAD internal procedures require that each remittance be supported by a list of employees with their monthly basic salary before we post their contributions to the PhilHealth system. Upon reviewing our records, we could not find any evidence of a remittance for December 1999, making it impossible for us to remit the claimed amount. 

The City plans to challenge the claim of PhilHealth due to the following reasons:

The amount provided by PhilHealth is based only on estimate and averaging.

Expiration of insurance premiums

The PhilHealth cannot provide a list of employees with amount of billings to substantiate their claim.

For GSIS – on September 11, 2023, CAD received the confirmation from GSIS regarding discrepancies in premium remittances for 1,768 employees, with a total unpaid remittance of P3,179,978.34 from October 2007 to 
June 2023. A total of P842,802.73 was resolved from 
CYs 2023 to 2024.

The CAD was able to determine the source of latest billings of GSIS based on the letter dated June 18, 2024 for the unremitted premiums. An amount of P744,010.12 for the premiums were validated as already remitted billings during year 2010 to year 2024. Additional remittances were made during CY 2024 amounting to P1,066,433.34.
The remaining amount from P3,425,052.63 billings is P1,614,609.17 which will be resolved through adjustments only of service records of employees in GSIS portal by the HRMDO.

For PhilHealth – the City plans to challenge the claim.

For Pag-IBIG – the CAD endorsed the letter of Pag-IBIG to COA for the purpose of seeking advice for appropriate action to be taken with regard to the dormant balance in Due to Pag-IBIG Fund given the fact that Pag-IBIG replied that the City has no outstanding balances except for the current billings.

For GSIS – the CAD and HRDMO are still in the process of resolving the variances in loans.

Strict monitoring of employees’ premium and loan deduction through participation of the remittance division in the conduct of pre-audit procedures of payroll.
Implemented a monthly monitoring of salary increment and newly/promoted employees through issuance of notice by the HRMDO with the CAD and respective payroll clerks.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Require the CTD and CAD to prepare a coordinated action plan containing the options and possible solutions to address the audit findings concerning the CIB accounts, and to consider the following:
Issue a follow-up request with the banks concerned for documents/information, copy furnished the head offices, for the possibility of securing related archived documents/information;
Intensify efforts in the verification/validation of the dormant accounts to see the possibility of closing the accounts. Consolidate the balances of the bank accounts with the same purpose/source of funding;
Reconcile the City’s records with the records of the source agencies regarding the dormant accounts under trust fund transferred from NGAs to facilitate the return of the balances to the source agencies concerned;
Ensure that the City's reported cash accounts are adequately backed up with confirmed bank balances and duly supported with appropriate documents, and timely address issues affecting the cash in bank accounts; and
Effect the necessary adjustment/s in the books of accounts, upon receipt of the documents or proof of transactions from the banks concerned and/or as warranted by transaction documents.
Establish an agreement with the banks to furnish the CTD with the monthly debit and credit memos for the timely recording of reconciling items; and
Require the CAD to prepare the BRS for all bank accounts in compliance with Sections 3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011.
We also recommended that Management require the CTD to:
Strictly monitor all bank accounts to avoid dormancy and escheat order/s, which are conditions affecting cash management; and
Apply for the reactivation of the account reported to the Treasurer of the Philippines as unclaimed balances pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 8, 2010 or the Uniform Rules and Procedures for Reactivation of Unclaimed Balances Reported to the Treasurer of the Philippines.
Further, we recommended that Management require the CTD to send written notice to the payee of the existence of the check at least one month before a check becomes stale, pursuant to Section 59, Chapter 3 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I.

City Legal Department (CLD) to recommend appropriate action to be taken in the case of AOs who are already separated from the service; and
The City Administrator to constantly remind officials and employees concerned of the pertinent laws, rules, and regulations governing the handling of cash advances every time they are granted with cash advance.

Design an efficient and effective disposal plan/schedule to cover all unserviceable properties and prevent them from remaining undisposed by year-end.
CAD to draw the corresponding JEV for the derecognition of the properties disposed of based on the accomplished IIRUP submitted by the GSD.
We recommended that Management require the GSD to conduct physical count of all PPE, including land, land improvements, infrastructure assets, buildings, and other structures, the results of which shall be reflected in the RPCPPE and reconciled with the accounting records; and CAD to maintain an updated property ledger cards for each PPE item, and the GSD to maintain the equivalent property cards to properly account for the receipt and disposition of each PPE item.
We also recommended that Management adopt and fully implement the “One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances” as authorized under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to address the unreconciled discrepancies between the accounting and property records, subject to full compliance with the general and procedural guidelines provided therein.
We enjoined the Management to continue to adhere with DPWH-DILG JMC No. 001 in the remittance of the 5% DPWH share on building permit fees.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to effect the necessary correcting entries to reflect the remittance of the P109,336.36 balance, and to consistently exercise due care in the recording of transactions.

We recommended that Management require the:
CTD to:
Monitor the funds due for remittance and remit them immediately in full to the BFP within the required period;
Strictly implement and comply with the provisions of Section 11, Rule IX of the IRR of RA No. 11032; and
CTD and CAD to formulate effective and efficient strategies to streamline the procedures of remitting FSIF collections and approval workflows.

We commended Management and its responsible officials for the prompt distribution and liquidation of HEA funds received in CY 2023 in compliance with the MOA between the City and the DOH, and in pursuance of the objectives of RA No. 11712 and DOH AO No. 2022-0039.
We recommended that Management maintain this practice by ensuring the full utilization of HEA funds upon receiving fund transfers from the DOH and its liquidation within the period prescribed by the MOA.

We recommended that Management:
Require the OSCA to coordinate with the Office of the Barangay Secretariat and the Barangay Officials of Caloocan City to enhance the dissemination of information regarding the scheduling of dates and venue of the SPISC distribution to qualified beneficiaries so as not to delay the payout and receipt of their benefit; and
Continuously seek suitable solutions to completely distribute the SPISC payouts to all eligible beneficiaries in full attainment of the objectives of the program.
We recommended that Management require the OSCA to submit the SPISC liquidation reports and supporting documents within the prescribed period provided in the MOA to facilitate the evaluation and validation of the SPISC distributions.

We commended Management for faithfully adhering to the provisions of Section 287 of RA No. 7160 and the related DILG-DBM joint issuances in implementing its planned socio-economic and environmental development projects.
As to the two unimplemented projects, we recommended that Management require the:
CED to prioritize the finalization of the design and the scope/program of works; and
City Planning and Development Department (CPDD), in coordination with the CED, to continue to monitor the implementation of the targeted development projects funded out of the 20% DF.

We recommended that Management continue to comply with the provisions of Section 1, Rule 18 of the IRR of RA No. 10121 in setting aside 5 percent of the budget intended for PPAs supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities.

We recommended that Management stays to be a vibrant lead for the timely and full implementation of its LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the purpose of the fund and to carry out its vision for an effective and efficient mechanism for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response rehabilitation and recovery, aligned with the dynamics of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 in pursuit of the objectives of Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

We recommended that the City GAD Focal Point closely coordinate with the implementing offices to consider in their succeeding proposals the challenges that affected their implementation in the past year to be able to fully implement programmed GAD PPAs.
We recommended that Management require the GAD Coordinating Office to submit the GPB and the GAD AR within the prescribed period to facilitate evaluation and validation of the GAD PPAs.

We recommended that Management require the CEMD and All Punong Barangays, to strictly enforce City Ordinance No. 0407, s. 2006, and intensify pro-active strategies and efforts in the implementation of the SWM programs of the City in accordance with Section 21 of RA No. 9003.

We acknowledged the efforts of Management and its responsible officials in assisting the establishment of MRF/MRS in every barangay within the City. Nevertheless, we recommended that Management, through the CEMD, implement the prescribed procedures and guidelines provided in RA No. 9003 for the establishment of MRFs in barangays and cluster of barangays that have not yet established them.

We recommended that Management immediately rectify the composition of the CCCPC, and ensure the involvement of all required Sectors as provided under RA No. 9344 and Item 5.2.2 of the DILG MC No. 2021-039, to ensure that all aspects affecting the welfare and rights of children are considered in the PPAs of the CCCPC; thus, increasing the effectiveness of the CCCPC in the performance of its defined functions, duties and responsibilities.

We recommended that Management require the CCCPC to:
Ensure that the AWFP embodies all PPAs on the protection of children, including those to be implemented by other offices of the City; and
Closely coordinate with the implementing offices to give priority to planning and preparation of budget proposals for PPAs outlined in the AWFP for LCPC.

We recommended that Management consistently perform meticulous planning and implementation of all identified and budgeted CCCPC PPAs to ensure that all aspects of child protection, including prevention, intervention, and support services, are thoroughly addressed, leaving no gaps in safeguarding children's rights and well-being, in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9344.

We recommended that Management regularly update the LCCAP, at least annually, as required under Item 2.3.5 of DILG MC No. 2014-135, to reflect the changing social, economic, and environmental conditions and emerging issues in the plans and programs of the City.

We recommended that the CCCCAP Core Team oversee the process of tagging climate-responsive PPAs in the LGU Plans and Investment Programs to ensure their proper identification and prioritization with the help of an expert/specialist on climate change from its members, or hired externally, and henceforth comply with the DBM-CCC-DILG MC No. 2015-01, dated July 23, 2015, in crafting the AIP using the CCET as guide.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Sustain demonstrated efforts in complying with the BIR regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes; and
Fast tract the review of outstanding balances of taxes withheld in prior years and the current year for further appropriate action and adjustment, as warranted.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management require the:
HRMDO to strengthen its record-keeping process in ensuring that employee data and membership records with GSIS, Pag-IBIG Fund and PhilHealth are updated accurately and timely. The employee data accuracy and integrity are integral to prevent future unremitted collections caused by incomplete or inconsistent record keeping.
CAD and HRMDO to undertake more extensive efforts to work back and reconcile the remaining unreconciled amounts, giving priority to the identified discrepancies provided by GSIS and PhilHealth in their responses; and
CAD to remit immediately the unremitted contributions and loan amortizations withheld from officers and employees to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG Fund and PhilHealth.

We recommended that Management, through the HRMDO, require the payroll clerks to strictly adhere to the monthly billing and Notice to Deduct provided by the CAD every month, as these serve as the basis for employees’ premium and loan deductions, ensuring accurate payroll data and preventing over-withholding from employees.

We recommended that Management direct the CED and the GSD to:
Strictly comply with the submission of copies of contracts/POs and notices of delivery together with the required supporting documents within the prescribed period pursuant to Section 39 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 96-010 to facilitate the review and evaluation of the contracts/POs and the conduct of inspection of delivered items; and
Design effective strategies to address the identified causes for the delayed submission of contracts/POs and notices of delivery.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:
Immediately submit all the identified DVs and LRs and the corresponding JEVs and supporting documents to the OCA for audit and safekeeping; and
Comply with the prescribed period of submission as required under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, to enable the OCA to fully execute its review and evaluation and to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension and imposition of sanction provided under Section 122 (2) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management require the CAD to strictly enforce the liquidation of CAs granted to Accountable Officers (AOs) within the reglementary period in compliance with Item 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, and impose the sanctions provided in Item 5.9 of the same COA Circular as warranted.

We recommended that Management require the

CAD to re-file the request for write-off for the amount of P4,817,495.00, strictly complying the requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005;

City Legal Officer to recommend to the City Mayor appropriate action to be taken for AOs who are already separated from the service; and

City Administrator to constantly remind concerned officials and employees on the pertinent laws, rules and regulations governing the handling of cash advance every time they are granted with cash advance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require the GSD to prepare the prescribed RPCPPE by category (i.e. Land and Land Improvements; Local Road Network; Other Public Infrastructures; Buildings and Other Structures; and Equipment, Furniture and Fixtures, and Other PPE) to facilitate reconciliation of the property and accounting records;

Require the CAD and the GSD to exercise proper coordination to reconcile the result of the physical count with the property and accounting records, and to make the necessary adjustments, as warranted;

Require the CAD to locate the documents relative to the unidentified PPE items to facilitate reconstruction of the remaining balances of the affected PPE accounts; and

Complete the updating of the composition of the Inventory Committee, and apply the One-time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances for Disposition and/or Derecognition of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs allowed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, subject to full compliance with the requirements under Items 7 and 8 thereof.

Non-derecognition of the undetermined value of replaced/ removed materials incident to the repairs, renovation and rehabilitation of 14 buildings and structures

We recommended that Management:

Create an appraisal committee from the offices/departments concerned to compute and determine the value of the removed/replaced costs of parts/materials that need to be derecognized in the books following the provisions of International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) and furnish COA a copy of the committee’s appraisal report;

Require the CAD to derecognize in the books, the costs of replaced/removed materials incident to the repairs, renovations and rehabilitations made based on the report of the appraisal committee;

Require the GSD to coordinate with the CAD regarding the dropping from the books of accounts the structures from the Old City Hall; 

Include as part of the standard operating procedure, the appraisal of costs of replaced/removed materials whenever there are repairs, renovations or rehabilitations made in any of the City’s buildings and structures; and

If the removed/ replaced parts/ materials have become unserviceable, immediately initiate disposal procedures as required under the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management for Local Governments.

Inaccurate computations of depreciation in the aggregate amount of P2.189 million

We recommended that Management require the CAD to:

Effect the necessary adjustments on the affected accounts; and

Set up an effective review level within the CAD so as to detect errors, inaccuracies and oversight in the accounting process to ensure accurate computation and recording of depreciation.  

Inclusion of unserviceable assets totaling P102.527 million in the books of accounts as of year-end. Moreover, the submitted RPCPPEs as of December 31, 2022 and the Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) as of July 31, 2022 were not completely accomplished, hence, affecting the reliability of the said reports.

We recommended that the 

City GSD to:

Conduct the inspection of the reported unserviceable properties and prepare the corresponding IIRUP thereon, after which, proceed with the disposal procedures in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992;

After the disposal, completely provide the necessary details on the Inspection and Disposal portion of the IIRUP, duly certified by the inspection officer and the witness, and submit the original copy of the report to the CAD for the preparation of the corresponding JEV for the derecognition of the book value of the properties from the books of accounts pursuant to Sections 194 and 195 of COA Circular No. 92-386;

Ensure that the RPCPPE as of year-end does not include unserviceable properties that were already disposed of; and

Use the prescribed Report on the Physical Count of Equipment, Furniture and Fixtures, and Other PPE (Appendix 66, Volume II of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs) which provides the complete information as to the description, property number, cost, location and condition of the properties; and

CAD to draw a JEV to adjust the accounts affected based on the properly accomplished IIRUP submitted by the GSD

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation to Management to require the City Accounting Department (CAD) to:
Coordinate with the City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) to secure the necessary documents or proof of transactions from the banks concerned to support the recording of the reconciling items; and
Conduct immediate and thorough verification of all outstanding reconciling items not yet taken up in the books to establish the correct balances of the CIB accounts and thereafter furnish the City Auditor’s Office with copies of Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) taking up the adjustments.

We reiterated our recommendation to the CAD and the CTO to exert full efforts in identifying and analyzing the possible cause of negative balances and effect the necessary adjusting journal entries in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Management require the CTO and CAD to:
Exert efforts in the verification/validation of these dormant accounts to see the possibility of closing these accounts and/or reclassifying them to other bank accounts under the same fund.  Consolidate the balances with the bank accounts for the same purpose/source of funding;
Reconcile the City’s records with the records of the Source Agencies (SAs) regarding the dormant accounts under trust fund transferred from NGAs to facilitate the return of the balances to the SAs concerned; and
Effect the necessary adjustment/s in the book of accounts, as warranted by transaction documents.

We recommended that the CAD further review its records and verify/reconcile with the bank regarding Account No. xxxx-xxxx-24 and make the necessary adjustment/s in the books.

We recommended that the Management require the CTD and the CAD to exert full efforts in identifying and analyzing the cash balances and effect the necessary adjusting journal entry in the books of accounts, as warranted.

We recommended that the Management require the:
CAD to prepare the BRS for all bank accounts in compliance with Sections 3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011;
CTO to request from the banks concerned, copies of bank statements of the City’s bank accounts; and
CAD and CTO to establish an effective coordination system so that the City’s cash accounts are adequately backed-up with confirmed bank balances and supported with appropriate documents.

We recommended that the Management exert effort in the validation and verification of these accounts and immediately transfer the bank account deposits from PNB to any of the AGDBs or may opt to obtain clearance/approval from the BLGF should the City wishes to retain maintenance of their current deposits with PNB.

We recommended that Management require the:
The CAD to:
Strictly adopt the accrual basis of accounting pursuant to IPSAS 1, which is the financial reporting framework adopted in government;
Review and analyze the claims and reimbursements to and by PhilHealth on hospital fees and charges for CY 2021 and prior years, and make the necessary adjusting journal entries, as warranted; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Section 21 of RA No. 11223.
The CCMC and the CCNMC to furnish the CAD copies of the hospital claims to PhilHealth for proper recording pursuant to and IPSAS 1 and Section 21 of RA No. 11223.

We recommended that the GSD and the CAD, in coordination with other departments/offices concerned, establish an effective process flow to address the noted gaps and reconcile their records for the necessary adjustments;

We recommended that the Management require the CAD to locate documents relative to the unidentified PPE items to facilitate reconstruction of the remaining balances of the said accounts.
We also recommended to Management, in the case of the unsettled PPE discrepancies after all efforts done, to evaluate if their case would qualify in the application for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances for Disposition of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs allowed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, subject to full compliance of the requirements under Item Nos. 7 and 8 thereof.
We recommended that the Management require the AMD–GSD to initiate immediate disposal to ensure fair valuation of the assets on the books.

We recommend that Management require:
The Inventory Committee to immediately prepare the IIRUP for approval of the City Mayor; and
The AMD–GSD to immediately submit to the CAD the approved IIRUP supported with pertinent documents for derecognition in the books.

We recommended that Management require the Inventory Committee to strictly conduct physical inventory of PPE pursuant to COA Circular 
No. 2020-006 to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the CAD coordinate further with the CED to identify the projects composing the unaccounted CIP accounts reported in 2013 and in prior years and locate the records/relevant documents pertaining thereto in their custody so that necessary adjustment could be made to the accounts affected.

We recommended that the Management requires the Inventory Committee to:
Prepare complete physical count report using the RPCPPE for all fixed assets, whether serviceable or not, and properly identify the asset’s condition on the “remarks” column;
Prepare the IIRUP for all unserviceable properties; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Management require the CAD to remit the P2,400,945.72 and comply strictly to Revenue Memorandum Circular 
No. 23-2007, to avoid penalties that may be imposed by the BIR.  Otherwise, the responsible official shall be held liable for the payment of penalties resulting from the delay as provided under the National Internal Revenue Code of 1997.

We recommended that the Management require the CAD to:
Ensure proper recording of the remittance of taxes withheld under the GF, SEF and TF; and
Revisit the existing procedures with the end in view of addressing the noted observations.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the CAD to:
Use the approved appropriations as the correct basis in calculating the unexpended balance of DRRMF for transfer to the special trust fund, pursuant to Section 21 of RA No. 10121; and
Effect the necessary adjustments in the GF and TF books and the memorandum entries for the Subsidiary Ledger accounts.

We recommended that the City requires the CCSWM Board to:
Prepare the annual progress report on the strategies implemented and accomplishments and submit the report to the National SWM Commission through its Secretariat; and
Update, on a yearly basis, the City’s SWM Plan in compliance with Section 16 of Republic Act 9003.

We recommended that the City, through the SWM Board, continue with the proper implementation of the project by assisting each barangay or cluster of barangays, specifically those with no MRF/MRSs yet, in providing or establishing an MRF that is designed in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9003 to attain the maximized gain aimed to be achieved by the law.

We recommended that Management maintains a more effective communication channel between project proponents and the City, requiring the former to inform the latter of updates and changes in the implementation of approved PPAs to forestall the cancellation of planned projects and utilize optimally the 20% Development Fund.

We recognized and commended the City’s efforts in executing the program and deploying much-needed financial assistance to its constituents in a swift and timely manner, embracing technology to provide effective delivery of services during these uncertain times amid a global health pandemic. We also recommended and Management agreed to continue leveraging information and digital technology, not just for an extraordinary program like the financial assistance program, but in other areas where applicable to better improve the delivery of goods and services to its constituency.

We recommend closer coordination with the agencies and personnel concerned as regards the requirements on the unremitted amounts in order to fully comply with RA No. 8291, RA No. 9679 and the IRR to 
RA No. 9241, and for the full benefit of the City personnel.
We also recommend that an effective system be established regarding the timely updating of personnel salary adjustments and submission of Member Identification Numbers of new employees.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive :
Strictly enforce compliance of audit suspensions and require persons responsible to submit the complete documentary requirements/justifications/explanations as required in the Notices of Suspensions; and
Enforce the immediate settlement of the audit disallowances and charges and initiate the necessary administrative and/or criminal action in case of unjustified failure/refusal to effect the settlement of disallowances and charges within the prescribed period in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended  that Management  properly plan the  development projects chargeable against the 20% City Development Projects to  
a)  avoid duplication with projects implemented by other  agencies  and other circumstances; b) maximize the use of the funds for its intended purposes; and c) enable the City  to further  provide the  needed services/facilities  to its constituents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the:
CAO and City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) to facilitate reconciliation of the cashbook, SL and bank balances and to ensure the completeness and accuracy of recorded transactions and balances of the Cash in Bank accounts;
CTO to -
follow up with the PNB on the latter’s the response to the confirmation letters pertaining to the existing balances, to facilitate reconciliation of records; and
request copies of debit and credit memos (DMs/CMs) from concerned banks and submit the same to the CAO for recording; and
CAO to request each depository bank to provide promptly the monthly bank statements to facilitate the preparation of BRSs, and effect the corresponding adjustments of the noted discrepancies, if warranted.

We recommended that Management require the:
CAO and GSO to –
abide strictly with the processes/procedures provided in Sections 51 and 121, Volumes 1 and 3, respectively, of the NGAS Manual for LGUs on the recording of procured supplies and materials;
maintain Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards as required under Sections 119 and 120, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, to ensure that each item is properly accounted for, thus, facilitating monitoring and recording and preparation of the RPCI; and
GSO to adhere strictly with the prescribed submission of the RPCI for the 1st and 2nd semesters to the Office of the City Auditor as provided under Section 124 Chapter 7 Volume 3 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, to facilitate the verification and validation of the reported balances of the Inventory accounts in the financial statements and reconciliation with the records of the CAO.

We recommended that Management require the :
GSO and CAO in coordination with various offices and departments to continuously reconcile their records and make the necessary adjustments;
CAO to prepare journal entry vouchers to correct and/or adjust the misclassified accounts to ensure the correctness and reliability of the carrying values of PPE account balances appearing in the financial statements; and
CTO to provide copies of the TCTs to the GSO to facilitate identification and location of the real estate properties of the City.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendations that the Management require the CAO to:
Analyze and conduct a thorough review of the transactions and send confirmation letters to check the existence of  claimants; and
If the transactions are not substantiated with the necessary documents and the payable accounts were found non-existing with unidentified valid claimants, adjustments should be made to the AP and Government Equity Accounts.

We recommended that Management require the CAO to reconcile its records with the records of the concerned NGAs in order to establish the correct and factual amounts in both records, thus, the liabilities and/or accountabilities of the City with the agencies, if any will be settled, if not eliminated.

We recommended that Management instruct the GSO and/or BAC to submit copies of the awarded contracts and the required supporting documents within the prescribed period  under COA Circular 2009-001, to facilitate the auditorial review and technical evaluation of the same, thus, any deficiencies noted would be relayed immediately to management for corrective measures.

We recommended that Management require the GSO to submit the Notices of Deliveries within the prescribed period as provided under Section 114 (d), COA Circular No. 92-386 and Item No. A.2 of COA Circular No. 96-010 to ensure immediate inspection of the same.
We recommended that Management require the SDOs and accountable officers to maintain cashbooks and/or similar records to monitor their accountabilities and facilitate reconciliation of cash balances.

We recommended that Management require the CAO to submit the JEVs, DVs and supporting documents within the prescribed period provided under Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, to ensure the prompt examination and audit of the transactions.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
CDC and other responsible officials to properly plan the development projects chargeable against the 20% CDF to avoid reprogramming and amendments of the budgeted PPAs contained in the AIP; and
City Engineering Office and other concerned offices to exert efforts to fully utilize the funds for intended PPAs to ensure the optimum utilization and use of the same.

We recommended that Management to:
Reorganize the CCSWMP to include Chairperson of the SK Federation and a representative from an NGO, whose principal purpose is to promote recycling and the protection of air and water quality,  in order that the requisite representations as required by RA No. 9003 in the formulation and implementation of the City’s solid waste management plans and programs are complied;  and
Oblige all the barangays to maintain their respective MRFs as mandated under RA No. 9003 to ensure proper waste disposal, among others.

We commend Management and the responsible officials for the almost maximum utilization and implementation of GAD funds and PPAs, nevertheless, we still advise the Management to continuously encourage its concerned officials to vie for the optimum use and implementation of the GAD Funds  and the PPAs.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to submit the GADAR and the GPB within the prescribed period to facilitate evaluation and validation of programmed projects and activities.

We recommended that Management expedite the establishment of resilient evacuation centers in accordance with the guidelines set forth by the DILG Memorandum to ensure the safety of victims of disaster and the efficiency of the City’s disaster rescue and recovery operations.

We reiterated the recommendations contained in the AARs for
CYs 2010 to 2012 that Management undertake immediate representations with the members of the SP to expedite the enactment of an Ordinance seeking the implementation of Section 326 of RA No. 7160, to ensure higher revenue for real property taxes to be utilized for the City’s PPAs.
We also recommended that Management require the City Assessor to update the number of RPUs of idle lands for CY 2013-2019 and submit the same to the Office of the City Auditor, for verification and validation purposes.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management require the CTO to prepare an aging of real property tax dues of delinquent taxpayers and enforce the distraint and/or auction on the said properties to ensure higher revenues to finance the City’s operations.

We recommended that Management require the CAO to:
Determine the composition of the prior year’s obligation and remit immediately to the BIR, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth the appropriate amount of contributions including the current and past due payables;
Adhere strictly with the agencies’ respective regulations on the prescribed periods of remittance of withheld taxes, contributions/loan payments to avoid payment of interests, non-granting of loans to officials and employees, and delays in the processing of claims or forfeiture of benefits due to the employees; and
Make representations with the concerned officials of the BIR, 
Pag-IBIG Fund, PhilHealth as regards the settlement of the prior year’s unremitted withheld taxes/contributions/payments to avoid the imposition of penalties and/or eliminate the liabilities.

We  recommended that Management require the City Accounting Office to:
Establish the composition of the prior years’ obligations and remit immediately to the GSIS the total amount including current due and payable to GSIS;
Strictly comply with the GSIS regulation on the prescribed period of remittance of the employees’ and employer’s contributions to avoid payment of interests, non-granting of loans to officials and employees, and delays in the processing of claims of benefits due to the employees; and
Meet and/or make representations with GSIS officials and resolve the issue on the unremitted prior years’ contributions/payments in order to eliminate the enormous balances in the books of accounts.

We have recommended  that Management:
Strictly enforce the submission of required documents on the suspended transactions and/or claims/payments to avoid the issuance of NDs; and
Demand for the immediate settlement of the disallowed transactions or require the persons liable to avail of the payment by installment as allowed in COA Resolution No. 2015-031.

We recommended that Management require:

a. The CAO and CTO to facilitate reconciliation of the cashbook and SL balances to ensure the completeness and accuracy of recorded transactions and balances of Cash in Bank accounts; and  

b. The CAO to request each depository bank to provide promptly the monthly bank statements to facilitate the preparation of BRS, and effect the corresponding adjustments of the noted discrepancies, if warranted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

      a. Create an  Inventory  Committee  that  will  conduct  an  accurate
         physical count and classification of all properties listed in  the
         RPCPPE; and


      b. Require the Inventory Committee to complete the reconciliation  of
         the physical count of PPE with the records of the General Services
         Office (GSO) and the CAO to arrive at the correct balances of  PPE
         accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that:


      a. The Officer-in-Charge (OIC), GSO and all other  concerned  offices
         take appropriate action to have all parcels of land owned  by  the
         City covered by the corresponding TCT to  substantiate  its  claim
         for ownership; and


      b. The CAO and the Inventory Committee, thru the GSO reconcile  their
         respective records to come up with an accurate and  reliable  Land
         account balance under the SEF.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management require
      the City Accountant to:

      a. Facilitate the tracing and research of relevant documents to serve
         as a basis in adjusting the prior  years’  identified  reconciling
         items in the respective reciprocal accounts;


      b.   Review/analyze   prior   year’s   transactions   pertaining   to
         unidentified discrepancies and take up the  necessary  adjustments
         to correct the balances of the affected accounts; and


      c. Conduct regular review and reconciliation of  reciprocal  accounts
         and effect  adjustments  of  reconciling  items  periodically,  as
         identified  and  substantiated,  to  prevent  existence   of   long
         outstanding unadjusted reconciling items.

We recommended that Management effect the transfer  of  ownership  of
      the land in the name of the City in accordance with the terms of  the
      Deed of Absolute Sale entered into by and between the  City  and  the
      Dai-Ichi Electronics and Manufacturing Corporation.

We recommended that the GSO renew the registration of all serviceable
      motor vehicles of  the  City  with  the  LTO  and  the  contracts  of
      insurance with the GSIS to avoid  additional  expenses  in  terms  of
      penalties and payment of damages to third party in case of  vehicular
      accidents; and  condemn, auction or repair  the  unserviceable  motor
      vehicles, where appropriate.

We recommended that:


      a. The City Treasurer ensure  adequate  bonding  of  all  accountable
         officers to conform with the requirements set forth under  Section
         305(f) of the Local Government Code of 1991, Section 101(2) of  PD
         1445 and Section 5.1 of National  Treasury  Circular  No.  02-2009
         dated August 6, 2009; and


      b. The City Treasurer discontinue the practice of assigning job order
         personnel to collect miscellaneous income  to  strengthen  control
         over cash.

In view of the above observations, we recommended the following:


      a. Require the Accountable Officers (AOs)  to  maintain  cashbook  as
         required under Paragraph  J,  Chapter  III  of  the  Revised  Cash
         Examination Manual; and


      b.  Require  the  Collecting  and  Liquidating  Officer  to  property
         accomplish Section D of the Report  of  Collections  and  Deposits
         (RCD) as required under Section 49 of NGAS Manual, Volume II.

We recommended that:


      a. The City Treasurer update  the  RPT  Account  Registers/Taxpayer’s
         index cards and  submit  the  corresponding  duly  certified  list
         showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due  and  collectible
         for the year to the City Accountant as basis in the setting up  of
         RPT/SET Receivables; and


      b. The CAO maintain SL for current and prior  year’s  account  for  a
         detailed record of the accounts.

We recommended that:


        a. Management set more realistic forecasting of revenue taking  into
           account prior years’ experience and other  relevant  factors  and
           statistics as aid in setting targets;





        b. The City Treasurer resume the issuance of  cash  tickets  to  all
           transient vendors of Districts I and II and collection of  rental
           of stall owners at Langaray Market in accordance with Section  314
           of the Updated Caloocan City Revenue Code;





        c. Require the City Assessor’s Office (CAsO) to immediately complete
           the updating of the City’s SFMV to facilitate the preparation and
           issuance of the general revision of real property assessments and
           classification as required under Section 212 and 219 of RA  7160,
           to generate additional income  necessary  to  fund  its  priority
           programs and projects for the benefit of its constituents; and





        d. The Administrator of the commercial complex:


           • Formulate or develop effective and  practical  strategies  that
             could enhance collections of all unpaid accounts; and


           • Effect the  imposition  of  penalties/surcharges  to  defaulting
             lessees as provided under the terms and conditions of the  lease
             contract to compel the lessees to pay their rentals promptly.

We recommended that the City Accountant facilitate the  submission
         of all paid disbursement vouchers and liquidation reports  to  the
         Auditor’s  Office  in  compliance  with  the  prescribed  mode  of
         submission pursuant to Section 6.05 of  COA  Circular  No.  95-006
         dated May 18, 1995.

To avoid accumulation of unliquidated cash advances, we recommended
        that Management instruct the CAO to:


        a. Demand immediate  settlement/liquidation  from  all  accountable
           officers with outstanding cash advances pursuant to COA Circular
           97-002 and strictly monitor the liquidation of cash advances;


        b. Initiate the filing of  appropriate  Administrative  Proceedings

          b. The City Accountant:


               • Facilitate the preparation and submission of  BRS  for  all
                 accounts maintained by the City with its  depository  banks
                 to prove the correctness of the cash in bank accounts;


               •  Retrieve  the  necessary  data/documents   for   immediate
                 recording in the general ledger and cashbook, of all
                 identified reconciling items;


               • Adjust  the  P20,000,000.00  terminated  time  deposit  and
                 investigate the disposition of the remaining P10,000,000.00
                 for proper adjustment as necessary; and


               • Verify/validate the reason on the existence  of  five  bank
                 accounts with negative balances and effect  adjustment,  as
                 needed.

          c. Management undertake a review of its policy in the maintenance
             of bank accounts and institute  proper  internal  control  and
             monitoring mechanism to avoid incidences of undetected  errors
             and fund mismanagement.

9. We have recommended-


          a. That Management:


               • Create an Inventory Committee who will conduct an  accurate
                 physical count of all properties present and being utilized
                 by the City in its operations;


               • Direct  the  Committee  to  reconcile  the  result  of  the
                 physical count of PPE  with  the  property  and  accounting
                 records;


               • Direct the City Accountant and Property Officer  to  adjust
                 their  respective  records  based   on   the   results   of
                 reconciliation made;


          b. That the CGSO:


               • Coordinate with the City Treasurer  in  securing  the  land
                 titles in favor of the City; and


               • Locate the  missing  property  equipment  and  require  the
                 concerned accountable officers to produce the equipment  or
                 to restitute based on the replacement cost.


          c. That the CGSO and the Accountant  facilitate  the  preparation
             and maintenance of property cards and PPE Ledger Cards for all
             PPE.

8. We recommended that the CAO continue to locate relevant  documents
          supporting the negative balance and unreclassified  dormant  items
          composing the CIP accounts; verify/validate the actual  status  of
          the  reported  on-going  projects   and   effect   the   necessary
          adjustments in the books of accounts, as warranted.

7. We recommended that the City Accountant:


          •  Facilitate the tracing and locating of  relevant  documents  to
            serve  as  basis  in  adjusting  the  prior  years’   identified
            reconciling items in the respective reciprocal accounts;


          •   Review/analyze  prior  years’   transactions   pertaining   to
            unidentified discrepancies and take up the necessary adjustments
            to correct the balances of the affected accounts; and


          •  Accordingly,  conduct  regular  review  and  reconciliation  of
            reciprocal accounts  and  effect  adjustments  periodically,  as
            identified and  substantiated,  to  prevent  existence  of  long
            outstanding unadjusted reconciling items.

7. We recommended that the City Accountant facilitate the maintenance
          of special accounts in the General Fund for  economic  enterprises
          as prescribed under Section 313 of RA 7160.
